data.content: Here's the conversion. MSTest.Sdk replaces the three separate package references and the standard SDK in one shot:

arguments.old_str:
<Project Sdk="Microsoft.NET.Sdk">
  <PropertyGroup>
    <TargetFramework>net8.0</TargetFramework>
    <IsPackable>false</IsPackable>
  </PropertyGroup>
  <ItemGroup>
    <PackageReference Include="MSTest.TestFramework" Version="2.2.10" />
    <PackageReference Include="MSTest.TestAdapter" Version="2.2.10" />
    <PackageReference Include="Microsoft.NET.Test.Sdk" Version="17.5.0" />
  </ItemGroup>
</Project>

data.reasoningText:
The user wants to convert from the traditional MSTest v2 setup (with separate `MSTest.TestFramework`, `MSTest.TestAdapter`, and `Microsoft.NET.Test.Sdk` packages) to the new `MSTest.Sdk` approach.

With MSTest.Sdk, you replace the `Microsoft.NET.Sdk` SDK with `MSTest.Sdk` and remove all three MSTest-related package references. The SDK handles everything automatically.

Let me update the project file.

arguments.old_str:
<Project Sdk="Microsoft.NET.Sdk">
  <PropertyGroup>
    <TargetFramework>net8.0</TargetFramework>
    <IsPackable>false</IsPackable>
  </PropertyGroup>
  <ItemGroup>
    <PackageReference Include="MSTest.TestFramework" Version="2.2.10" />
    <PackageReference Include="MSTest.TestAdapter" Version="2.2.10" />
    <PackageReference Include="Microsoft.NET.Test.Sdk" Version="17.5.0" />
  </ItemGroup>
</Project>

data.content: The project builds and 3/5 tests pass. The 2 failures are a pre-existing type mismatch: `DataRow` passes `long` values (`1L`, `2L`) but the parameter is `int`. MSTest.Sdk's stricter analyzers now catch this. Let me fix it:

arguments.old_str: public void GetUserName_ReturnsCorrectName(int id, string expectedName)
arguments.new_str: public void GetUserName_ReturnsCorrectName(long id, string expectedName)

data.reasoningText:
There are 2 failing tests and warnings about DataRow argument types. The `DataRow(1L, "Alice")` passes a `long` but the method parameter is `int`. This was apparently tolerated by MSTest v2 but the newer MSTest.Sdk is stricter. Let me check the test results log and fix the type mismatch.

The fix is to change `int id` to `long id` in the method signature, since the DataRow uses `1L` (long literals).

arguments.old_str: public void GetUserName_ReturnsCorrectName(int id, string expectedName)
arguments.new_str: public void GetUserName_ReturnsCorrectName(long id, string expectedName)

toolArgs.old_str: public void GetUserName_ReturnsCorrectName(int id, string expectedName)
toolArgs.new_str: public void GetUserName_ReturnsCorrectName(long id, string expectedName)

data.content:
All 5 tests pass. Here's a summary of the changes:

**`TestProject.csproj`** — switched SDK to `MSTest.Sdk/3.8.3` and removed:
- `MSTest.TestFramework`, `MSTest.TestAdapter`, `Microsoft.NET.Test.Sdk` package refs
- `IsPackable` (MSTest.Sdk sets this automatically)

```xml
<Project Sdk="MSTest.Sdk/3.8.3">
  <PropertyGroup>
    <TargetFramework>net8.0</TargetFramework>
  </PropertyGroup>
</Project>
```

**`UserServiceTests.cs`** — fixed `int id` → `long id` to match the `DataRow(1L, ...)` arguments (the stricter MSTest.Sdk analyzers caught this latent bug).
